benchmark: Use latitude and longitude coordinates to understand the relationship between people and places on the Earth.
item_specs: Students use coordinate systems to locate places and describe spatial relationships.
essential_questions: How do we describe the location of places on Earth?
Why is the grid system important for navigation?
learning_targets: I can use latitude and longitude to find locations.
I can explain the purpose of the coordinate system.
I can interpret maps using grid coordinates.
sample_assessment: The line of latitude at 0 degrees is called the: A) Prime Meridian B) Equator C) Tropic of Cancer D) International Date Line

benchmark: Analyze the purposes of map projections (political, physical, special purpose) and explain the applications of various types of maps.
item_specs: Students identify different map types and analyze their purposes and limitations.
essential_questions: Why do different maps show different information?
What are the limitations of flat maps?
learning_targets: I can identify and compare different types of maps.
I can explain the purpose of political, physical, and special purpose maps.
I can analyze the strengths and limitations of map projections.
sample_assessment: Which type of map would be BEST for showing the elevation of a mountain range? A) Political map B) Physical map C) Road map D) Climate map

benchmark: Identify natural wonders of the ancient world.
item_specs: Students identify and locate natural wonders and landmarks of the ancient world.
essential_questions: What are the natural wonders of the ancient world?
How did natural features influence ancient civilizations?
learning_targets: I can identify natural wonders of the ancient world.
I can locate natural wonders on a map.
I can explain how natural features influenced ancient peoples.
sample_assessment: Which of the following is considered a natural wonder of the ancient world? A) The Colosseum B) The Great Wall of China C) The Grand Canyon D) The Pyramids of Giza

benchmark: Utilize tools geographers use to study the world.
item_specs: Students identify and use various geographic tools including maps, globes, and GIS.
essential_questions: What tools do geographers use to study Earth?
How has technology changed geographic study?
learning_targets: I can identify different types of maps and their purposes.
I can use map scales and legends.
I can explain how GIS technology helps geographers.
sample_assessment: A thematic map would BEST be used to show: A) Physical features B) Political boundaries C) Population density patterns D) Street directions

benchmark: Use scale, cardinal, and intermediate directions, and estimation of distances between places on current and ancient maps of the world.
item_specs: Students use map elements to determine direction and estimate distance.
essential_questions: How do we use scale to measure distance on a map?
What are cardinal and intermediate directions?
learning_targets: I can use a map scale to estimate distances.
I can identify cardinal and intermediate directions.
I can apply map skills to both ancient and modern maps.
sample_assessment: On a map with a scale of 1 inch = 100 miles, two cities that are 3 inches apart are approximately how far from each other? A) 100 miles B) 200 miles C) 300 miles D) 400 miles

benchmark: Use a map to identify major bodies of water of the world, and explain ways they have impacted the development of civilizations.
item_specs: Students locate major bodies of water and analyze their influence on human settlement and trade.
essential_questions: How have major bodies of water influenced civilizations?
Why did ancient civilizations develop near water?
learning_targets: I can locate major oceans, seas, and rivers on a world map.
I can explain how bodies of water influenced trade and settlement.
I can analyze the relationship between water access and civilization development.
sample_assessment: Which body of water was MOST important to trade in the ancient Mediterranean world? A) Atlantic Ocean B) Mediterranean Sea C) Pacific Ocean D) Indian Ocean

benchmark: Use maps to identify characteristics and boundaries of ancient civilizations that have shaped the world today.
item_specs: Students use historical maps to identify the locations and boundaries of ancient civilizations.
essential_questions: Where were the major ancient civilizations located?
How have ancient boundaries shaped the modern world?
learning_targets: I can locate ancient civilizations on a map.
I can identify the geographic boundaries of ancient empires.
I can compare ancient and modern political boundaries.
sample_assessment: The ancient civilization of Mesopotamia was located between which two rivers? A) Nile and Congo B) Tigris and Euphrates C) Ganges and Indus D) Yellow and Yangtze

benchmark: Explain how major physical characteristics, natural resources, climate, and absolute and relative locations have influenced settlement, interactions, and the economies of ancient civilizations of the world.
item_specs: Students analyze relationships between physical geography and human development.
essential_questions: How does geography influence where people live?
What role do natural resources play in economic development?
learning_targets: I can explain how physical features influenced settlement patterns.
I can analyze the relationship between resources and economic development.
I can describe how climate affected ancient civilizations.
sample_assessment: Ancient Egyptian civilization developed along the Nile River primarily because: A) The river provided transportation to other continents B) Annual floods deposited fertile soil for farming C) The river protected them from all invaders D) The Nile was the largest river in the world

benchmark: Differentiate between continents, regions, countries, and cities in order to understand the complexities of regions created by civilizations.
item_specs: Students classify geographic areas by type and analyze regional characteristics.
essential_questions: How do we organize the world into different geographic units?
What makes a region different from a country?
learning_targets: I can distinguish between continents, regions, countries, and cities.
I can identify the characteristics that define a region.
I can explain how civilizations created complex regions.
sample_assessment: Which is the BEST example of a cultural region? A) The continent of Africa B) The country of France C) The Middle East D) The city of Rome

benchmark: Analyze the relationship of physical geography to the development of ancient river valley civilizations.
item_specs: Students analyze how rivers and physical geography enabled the development of early civilizations.
essential_questions: Why did early civilizations develop in river valleys?
How did rivers support the growth of agriculture?
learning_targets: I can identify the major river valley civilizations.
I can explain how rivers supported agriculture and trade.
I can analyze the connection between geography and civilization development.
sample_assessment: The development of agriculture in river valleys led to: A) Decreased population B) Nomadic lifestyles C) Food surpluses and the growth of cities D) Isolation from other civilizations

benchmark: Explain how the geographical location of ancient civilizations contributed to the culture and politics of those societies.
item_specs: Students connect geographic location to cultural and political developments in ancient societies.
essential_questions: How did location shape the culture of ancient civilizations?
How did geography influence political structures?
learning_targets: I can explain how geography influenced cultural practices.
I can describe how location affected political development.
I can analyze the relationship between geography and government forms.
sample_assessment: The mountainous terrain of ancient Greece contributed to the development of: A) A unified empire B) Independent city-states C) A single religion D) Large-scale farming

benchmark: Interpret how geographic boundaries invite or limit interaction with other regions and cultures.
item_specs: Students analyze how geographic features serve as bridges or barriers between cultures.
essential_questions: How do geographic features connect or separate cultures?
What role do natural boundaries play in cultural exchange?
learning_targets: I can identify geographic features that serve as boundaries.
I can explain how geography can invite or limit cultural interaction.
I can analyze examples of geographic barriers and bridges.
sample_assessment: The Sahara Desert served as a geographic barrier that: A) Connected North Africa to sub-Saharan Africa easily B) Limited interaction between North and sub-Saharan Africa C) Had no effect on trade D) Encouraged migration southward

benchmark: Explain the concept of cultural diffusion, and identify the influences of different ancient cultures on one another. The ancient Silk Road: geography and transportation (6-G.1) The ancient Silk Road: goods and ideas (6-G.2) Pax Romana (6-J.8) Roman legacies (6-J.11)
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to analyze and explain the interactions between different ancient cultures, focusing on the Silk Road's role in cultural diffusion. Items may include scenario-based questions, requiring students to identify specific examples of cultural exchange and their impacts.
essential_questions: How did the Silk Road facilitate cultural exchange among ancient civilizations?
What are some examples of cultural diffusion that occurred due to trade?
In what ways did the Pax Romana influence cultural interactions?
learning_targets: I can explain the concept of cultural diffusion.
I can identify influences of different ancient cultures on one another.
I can describe the significance of the Silk Road in ancient trade.
sample_assessment: Which of the following best describes how the Silk Road contributed to cultural diffusion among ancient civilizations?

benchmark: Interpret choropleths or dot-density maps to explain the distribution of population in the ancient world.
item_specs: Assessment items should involve interpreting choropleth and dot-density maps that depict population distribution in the ancient world. Students should analyze data presented in maps and answer questions that require them to draw conclusions based on visual information.
essential_questions: What patterns can be observed in the population distribution of ancient civilizations?
How do maps help us understand historical demographics?
What factors might have influenced population distribution in the ancient world?
learning_targets: I can interpret choropleth maps to analyze population distribution.
I can explain the significance of population density in ancient societies.
I can compare different regions based on their population data.
sample_assessment: Based on the choropleth map provided, which region had the highest population density in the ancient world?

benchmark: Explain how the physical landscape has affected the development of agriculture and industry in the ancient world. Early Mesopotamia (6-B.1) Ancient Egypt: the Old Kingdom (6-C.1) Ancient China (6-D.1) The Indus Civilization (6-E.1) Compare ancient river civilizations: geography and society (6-F.1)
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to analyze how the physical landscape influenced agricultural and industrial development in various ancient civilizations. Items may include case studies or comparative analyses of different regions and their adaptations to their environments.
essential_questions: How did the geography of ancient Mesopotamia support agriculture?
In what ways did the physical landscape shape the industries of ancient Egypt?
What similarities and differences can be found in the agricultural practices of ancient river civilizations?
learning_targets: I can explain how geography affected agriculture in ancient civilizations.
I can analyze the relationship between the physical landscape and industry.
I can compare agricultural practices across different ancient cultures.
sample_assessment: How did the physical landscape of the Indus Civilization contribute to its agricultural practices?

benchmark: Analyze the impact of human populations on the ancient world's ecosystems.
item_specs: Assessment items should challenge students to analyze the effects of human populations on ancient ecosystems. Items may involve evaluating case studies or historical scenarios where human activity significantly altered the environment, requiring critical thinking and synthesis of information.
essential_questions: What were the ecological impacts of human settlements in ancient civilizations?
How did agriculture change the ecosystems of ancient societies?
In what ways did human activity contribute to environmental changes in the ancient world?
learning_targets: I can analyze the impact of human populations on ecosystems.
I can evaluate historical examples of human-environment interactions.
I can discuss the consequences of agricultural practices on ancient ecosystems.
sample_assessment: Which of the following best describes a significant impact of agriculture on the ecosystems of ancient civilizations?

benchmark: Explain how family and ethnic relationships influenced ancient cultures.
item_specs: Assessment items should focus on explaining how family and ethnic relationships influenced cultural practices and societal structures in ancient cultures. Items may include identification of key relationships and their effects on social norms and traditions.
essential_questions: How did family structures shape the cultures of ancient societies?
What role did ethnic diversity play in the development of ancient cultures?
In what ways did family and ethnic relationships influence governance in ancient civilizations?
learning_targets: I can explain the influence of family relationships on ancient cultures.
I can identify the impact of ethnic diversity in historical societies.
I can discuss how family and ethnic ties affected social structures.
sample_assessment: Which statement best describes the influence of family relationships on the culture of ancient Rome?

benchmark: Use maps to trace significant migrations, and analyze their results.
item_specs: Students use maps to trace migration patterns and analyze their causes and effects.
essential_questions: Why do people migrate?
How have migrations shaped the ancient world?
learning_targets: I can trace migration routes on a map.
I can identify push and pull factors of migration.
I can analyze the results of significant migrations.
sample_assessment: A push factor for migration would be: A) Better farmland B) Job opportunities C) Drought and famine D) Religious freedom

benchmark: Locate sites in Africa and Asia where archaeologists have found evidence of early human societies, and trace their migration patterns to other parts of the world. The Neolithic Period (6-A.1)
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to identify and locate key archaeological sites in Africa and Asia, as well as trace migration patterns using maps and timelines. Items may include multiple-choice questions, short answer responses, and map analysis tasks.
essential_questions: What evidence do archaeologists use to understand early human societies?
How did early human migration patterns shape the world?
What factors influenced the movement of early humans?
learning_targets: I can locate important archaeological sites in Africa and Asia.
I can trace the migration patterns of early human societies.
I can explain the significance of the Neolithic Period.
sample_assessment: Which of the following regions is known for significant archaeological findings related to early human societies? A) Amazon Rainforest B) Sahara Desert C) Great Plains D) Himalayan Mountains

benchmark: Map and analyze the impact of the spread of various belief systems in the ancient world. The teachings of Confucius (6-D.6) The ancient Silk Road: goods and ideas (6-G.2) Origins of Judaism (6-H.1) Origins of Christianity (6-H.2) Origins of Islam (6-H.3) Origins of Hinduism (6-H.4) Origins of Buddhism (6-H.5) Early Islamic conquests (6-K.1)
item_specs: Assessment items should encourage students to map the spread of various belief systems and analyze their impacts on ancient civilizations. Items may include case studies, map interpretation, and comparative analysis questions.
essential_questions: How did belief systems influence ancient societies?
What role did the Silk Road play in the exchange of ideas?
In what ways did the teachings of Confucius shape Chinese culture?
learning_targets: I can map the spread of major belief systems in the ancient world.
I can analyze the impact of the Silk Road on trade and culture.
I can compare the origins of different religions.
sample_assessment: Which belief system originated in the Middle East and emphasizes monotheism? A) Buddhism B) Hinduism C) Judaism D) Confucianism

benchmark: Identify the methods used to compensate for the scarcity of resources in the ancient world.
item_specs: Assessment items should focus on identifying methods used by ancient civilizations to address resource scarcity. Items may include multiple-choice questions, short answer explanations, and scenario-based questions.
essential_questions: What strategies did ancient civilizations use to manage scarce resources?
How did resource scarcity affect the development of societies?
What innovations arose in response to resource limitations?
learning_targets: I can identify methods used to compensate for resource scarcity.
I can explain the impact of resource availability on ancient civilizations.
I can analyze how scarcity influenced societal development.
sample_assessment: Which of the following was a common method used by ancient civilizations to deal with resource scarcity? A) Importing goods B) Increasing taxes C) Expanding territory D) Reducing population

benchmark: Use geographic terms and tools to explain why ancient civilizations developed networks of highways, waterways, and other transportation linkages.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to use geographic terms and tools to explain the development of transportation networks in ancient civilizations. Items may include map analysis, short answer questions, and project-based assessments.
essential_questions: Why were transportation networks crucial for ancient civilizations?
How did geography influence the development of trade routes?
What geographic features facilitated the growth of highways and waterways?
learning_targets: I can explain the importance of transportation networks in ancient civilizations.
I can analyze how geography shaped trade and communication.
I can identify key geographic features that influenced transportation.
sample_assessment: Which geographic feature most likely influenced the development of trade routes in ancient civilizations? A) Mountains B) Deserts C) Rivers D) Forests

benchmark: Use geographic tools and terms to analyze how famine, drought, and natural disasters plagued many ancient civilizations.
item_specs: Assessment items should focus on analyzing the impact of natural disasters on ancient civilizations using geographic tools and terms. Items may include case studies, map analysis, and comparative questions.
essential_questions: How did natural disasters affect the stability of ancient civilizations?
What were the common responses of societies to famine and drought?
In what ways did geography contribute to the vulnerability of civilizations to disasters?
learning_targets: I can analyze the effects of famine and drought on ancient societies.
I can use geographic tools to assess the impact of natural disasters.
I can explain how ancient civilizations adapted to environmental challenges.
sample_assessment: Which of the following natural disasters is most likely to lead to famine in an ancient civilization? A) Earthquake B) Flood C) Drought D) Volcanic eruption

benchmark: Describe the Six Essential Elements of Geography (The World in Spatial Terms, Places and Regions, Physical Systems, Human Systems, Environment, The Uses of Geography) as the organizing framework for understanding the world and its people.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to identify and explain the Six Essential Elements of Geography, providing examples for each element. Items may include multiple-choice questions, short answer prompts, and matching exercises to assess understanding.
essential_questions: What are the Six Essential Elements of Geography?
How do these elements help us understand our world?
In what ways do these elements interact with each other?
learning_targets: I can identify the Six Essential Elements of Geography.
I can explain the significance of each element.
I can provide examples of how these elements relate to real-world scenarios.
sample_assessment: Which of the following is NOT one of the Six Essential Elements of Geography? A) Places and Regions B) Human Systems C) Climate D) Economic Systems

benchmark: Compare maps of the world in ancient times with current political maps.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to analyze and compare ancient maps with current political maps, focusing on differences in geographical representation and political boundaries. Items may include visual analysis, short essays, and multiple-choice questions.
essential_questions: How do ancient maps differ from modern political maps?
What can we learn about a civilization by studying its maps?
In what ways do maps reflect the political and cultural context of their time?
learning_targets: I can compare ancient maps to modern political maps.
I can identify key differences in geographical representation.
I can explain the significance of map changes over time.
sample_assessment: Which of the following best describes a key difference between ancient maps and modern political maps? A) Ancient maps are more accurate B) Modern maps include political boundaries C) Ancient maps show only physical features D) Modern maps do not show cultural regions

benchmark: Locate the fifty states and their capital cities in addition to the nation's capital on a map.
item_specs: Students locate U.S. states and capitals on a map.
essential_questions: Where are the fifty states located?
What are the capital cities of each state?
learning_targets: I can locate all fifty states on a U.S. map.
I can identify the capital city of each state.
I can locate Washington, D.C., on a map.
sample_assessment: Which city is the capital of the United States? A) New York City B) Los Angeles C) Washington, D.C. D) Philadelphia

benchmark: Locate on a world map the territories and protectorates of the United States of America.
item_specs: Students identify and locate U.S. territories and protectorates on a world map.
essential_questions: What territories does the United States hold?
Where are U.S. territories located in the world?
learning_targets: I can identify U.S. territories and protectorates.
I can locate U.S. territories on a world map.
I can explain the relationship between territories and the federal government.
sample_assessment: Which of the following is a U.S. territory? A) Cuba B) Puerto Rico C) Mexico D) Canada

benchmark: Interpret maps to identify geopolitical divisions and boundaries of places in North America.
item_specs: Students interpret maps to identify political boundaries and divisions in North America.
essential_questions: How are political boundaries shown on maps?
What geopolitical divisions exist in North America?
learning_targets: I can identify geopolitical boundaries on a map of North America.
I can describe the political divisions of the United States.
I can interpret maps showing counties, states, and national boundaries.
sample_assessment: Geopolitical boundaries are MOST often determined by: A) Physical features only B) Cultural and political agreements C) Random lines on a map D) Climate patterns

benchmark: Locate major cultural landmarks that are emblematic of the United States.
item_specs: Students identify and locate major cultural landmarks of the United States.
essential_questions: What are the major cultural landmarks of the United States?
Why are these landmarks important to American identity?
learning_targets: I can identify major cultural landmarks of the United States.
I can locate these landmarks on a map.
I can explain the significance of national landmarks.
sample_assessment: Which landmark is located in New York Harbor and symbolizes freedom and immigration? A) Mount Rushmore B) The Liberty Bell C) The Statue of Liberty D) The Lincoln Memorial

benchmark: Locate major physical landmarks that are emblematic of the United States.
item_specs: Students identify and locate major physical landmarks of the United States.
essential_questions: What are the major physical landmarks of the United States?
How have physical landmarks shaped American identity?
learning_targets: I can identify major physical landmarks of the United States.
I can locate physical landmarks on a U.S. map.
I can explain how physical features have influenced American settlement.
sample_assessment: Which physical landmark forms a natural boundary between several U.S. states and flows into the Gulf of Mexico? A) The Rocky Mountains B) The Mississippi River C) The Great Lakes D) The Grand Canyon

benchmark: Explain how major physical characteristics, natural resources, climate, and absolute and relative location have influenced settlement, economies, and inter-governmental relations in North America.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to synthesize information about how physical characteristics, natural resources, and climate influence settlement and economies. Items may include case studies, research projects, and analytical essays.
essential_questions: How do physical characteristics influence where people choose to settle?
What role do natural resources play in the economy of a region?
How does climate affect inter-governmental relations in North America?
learning_targets: I can explain how physical characteristics influence settlement patterns.
I can analyze the impact of natural resources on economies.
I can evaluate the effects of climate on inter-governmental relations.
sample_assessment: Which factor most significantly influences where people settle in North America? A) Climate B) Political boundaries C) Cultural practices D) Historical events

benchmark: Describe current major cultural regions of North America.
item_specs: Assessment items should focus on identifying and describing the major cultural regions of North America, including their characteristics and significance. Items may include multiple-choice questions, short answer prompts, and map labeling exercises.
essential_questions: What are the major cultural regions of North America?
How do cultural regions reflect the diversity of the population?
In what ways do cultural regions influence social and economic interactions?
learning_targets: I can identify the major cultural regions of North America.
I can describe the characteristics of each cultural region.
I can explain the significance of cultural diversity in North America.
sample_assessment: Which of the following is a major cultural region of North America? A) The Sahara Desert B) The Midwest C) The Amazon Rainforest D) The Himalayas

benchmark: Use maps to describe the location, abundance, and variety of natural resources in North America.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to use maps to analyze the location, abundance, and variety of natural resources in North America. Items may include map interpretation, data analysis, and short essays.
essential_questions: How are natural resources distributed across North America?
What factors influence the abundance of natural resources in different regions?
How do maps help us understand the availability of natural resources?
learning_targets: I can use maps to identify natural resources in North America.
I can analyze the abundance of resources in different regions.
I can describe the variety of natural resources found in North America.
sample_assessment: Which of the following natural resources is most abundant in the western region of North America? A) Oil B) Coal C) Timber D) Freshwater

benchmark: Use geographic terms and tools to explain cultural diffusion throughout North America.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to analyze maps, diagrams, or case studies that illustrate cultural diffusion. Items may include multiple-choice questions that ask students to identify examples of cultural diffusion or to explain the significance of geographic tools in understanding cultural exchanges.
essential_questions: How do geographic tools help us understand cultural diffusion?
What are some examples of cultural diffusion in North America?
In what ways does geography influence cultural interactions?
learning_targets: I can identify examples of cultural diffusion in North America.
I can explain how geographic tools are used to study cultural diffusion.
I can analyze the impact of geography on cultural exchanges.
sample_assessment: Which of the following best describes an example of cultural diffusion in North America?

benchmark: Use maps and other geographic tools to examine the importance of demographics within political divisions of the United States.
item_specs: Assessment items should focus on the interpretation of demographic data presented in maps or charts. Students should be asked to analyze the implications of demographic trends on political divisions, with items that require them to draw conclusions based on the data presented.
essential_questions: How do demographics influence political divisions in the United States?
What tools can be used to analyze demographic data?
Why is it important to understand demographics in political contexts?
learning_targets: I can analyze demographic data using geographic tools.
I can explain the relationship between demographics and political divisions.
I can interpret maps to understand demographic trends.
sample_assessment: What demographic factor is most likely to influence voting patterns in a given political division?

benchmark: Use a choropleth or other map to geographically represent current information about issues of conservation or ecology in the local community.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to interpret choropleth maps or other visual representations of ecological data. Items may include questions that ask students to identify areas of concern or to evaluate the effectiveness of conservation efforts based on the data presented.
essential_questions: How can maps help us understand local ecological issues?
What current conservation issues are represented in our community?
Why is it important to visually represent ecological data?
learning_targets: I can use maps to identify conservation issues in my community.
I can explain the significance of ecological data representation.
I can analyze choropleth maps to draw conclusions about local ecology.
sample_assessment: Which area on the choropleth map indicates the highest level of conservation concern?

benchmark: Use Geographic Information Systems (GIS) or other technology to view maps of current information about the United States.
item_specs: Assessment items should focus on the application of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) to analyze current data about the United States. Items may include scenarios where students must interpret GIS data to make informed decisions or predictions about geographical trends.
essential_questions: How does GIS technology enhance our understanding of geographic data?
What types of information can be analyzed using GIS?
In what ways can GIS impact decision-making in geography?
learning_targets: I can use GIS to analyze current geographic information.
I can explain the benefits of using technology in geography.
I can interpret data from GIS to make informed conclusions.
sample_assessment: What advantage does GIS provide when analyzing geographic data compared to traditional maps?

benchmark: Use maps to describe the physical and cultural attributes of major regions of North America.
item_specs: Students use maps to identify and describe physical and cultural characteristics of North American regions.
essential_questions: What are the major regions of North America?
How do physical and cultural features define regions?
learning_targets: I can identify major regions of North America on a map.
I can describe the physical features of each region.
I can explain how culture varies across regions.
sample_assessment: Which U.S. region is characterized by fertile plains and large-scale agriculture? A) New England B) The Midwest C) The Pacific Coast D) The Southeast

benchmark: Use appropriate geographic tools and terms to identify and describe significant places and regions in American history.
item_specs: Students use geographic tools to analyze places significant in American history.
essential_questions: How did geography shape key events in American history?
What geographic tools help us understand the past?
learning_targets: I can use maps and other tools to identify historically significant places.
I can describe the geographic context of major events in American history.
I can explain how geography influenced historical outcomes.
sample_assessment: The geographic feature that MOST influenced westward expansion was: A) The Appalachian Mountains B) The Mississippi River and flat Great Plains C) The Pacific Ocean D) The Great Lakes

benchmark: Identify the physical elements and the human elements that define and differentiate regions as relevant to American history.
item_specs: Students identify physical and human characteristics that define U.S. regions in historical context.
essential_questions: What physical and human elements define a region?
How did regional differences shape American history?
learning_targets: I can identify physical elements that define regions.
I can identify human elements that differentiate regions.
I can explain how regional differences influenced American history.
sample_assessment: Which factor MOST contributed to the economic differences between the Northern and Southern states before the Civil War? A) Climate and geography B) Population size C) Access to the ocean D) Proximity to Canada

benchmark: Use geographic terms and tools to analyze case studies of regional issues in different parts of the United States that have had critical economic, physical, or political ramifications.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to engage in higher-order thinking by analyzing case studies of regional issues. Items may ask students to evaluate the economic, physical, or political ramifications of these issues and to propose solutions based on their analysis.
essential_questions: What are the critical regional issues affecting different parts of the United States?
How do economic and political factors interact in regional issues?
What solutions can be proposed for addressing regional challenges?
learning_targets: I can analyze case studies of regional issues in the U.S.
I can evaluate the economic and political impacts of these issues.
I can propose solutions to regional challenges based on my analysis.
sample_assessment: Which of the following best describes the economic impact of the regional issue presented in the case study?

benchmark: Use geographic terms and tools to analyze case studies of how selected regions of the United States have changed over time.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to analyze case studies using geographic tools and terms, focusing on how specific regions in the United States have transformed over time. Items may include map analysis, data interpretation, and comparative essays.
essential_questions: How have geographic factors influenced the development of regions in the United States?
What tools can we use to assess changes in a region over time?
In what ways do case studies help us understand geographic change?
learning_targets: I can identify key geographic terms related to regional changes.
I can analyze case studies to understand historical transformations.
I can use geographic tools to illustrate changes in regions.
sample_assessment: Which geographic tool would best help analyze the population growth in a specific U.S. region over the last century?

benchmark: Locate and describe in geographic terms the major ecosystems of the United States.
item_specs: Assessment items should focus on locating and describing major ecosystems in the United States using geographic terms. Items may include map labeling, ecosystem identification, and short answer questions.
essential_questions: What are the major ecosystems found in the United States?
How do geographic features influence the distribution of ecosystems?
In what ways do ecosystems interact with human activities?
learning_targets: I can locate major ecosystems on a map of the United States.
I can describe the characteristics of different ecosystems.
I can explain the relationship between geography and ecosystems.
sample_assessment: Which of the following ecosystems is primarily found in the southeastern United States? A) Desert B) Tundra C) Wetlands D) Grasslands

benchmark: Use geographic terms and tools to explain differing perspectives on the use of renewable and non-renewable resources in the United States and Florida over time.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to use geographic terms and tools to explain differing perspectives on renewable and non-renewable resources. Items may include debates, case studies, and analysis of resource management.
essential_questions: What are the differing perspectives on resource use in the U.S.?
How do geographic factors influence resource management decisions?
What role do renewable resources play in sustainable development?
learning_targets: I can explain the differences between renewable and non-renewable resources.
I can analyze perspectives on resource use in the U.S.
I can use geographic tools to illustrate resource distribution.
sample_assessment: Which statement best represents a perspective on renewable resources? A) They are finite and will run out. B) They can be replenished naturally. C) They are more expensive to use. D) They cause environmental harm.

benchmark: Interpret population density maps and other data to analyze demographic patterns in North America.
item_specs: Students analyze population data and maps to identify demographic patterns.
essential_questions: What patterns exist in North American population distribution?
What factors influence where people settle?
learning_targets: I can interpret population density maps.
I can identify patterns in population distribution.
I can analyze factors that influence settlement patterns.
sample_assessment: Population density is HIGHEST in areas that: A) Have harsh climates B) Are near water and transportation C) Are mountainous D) Have limited resources

benchmark: Use geographic terms and tools to analyze the effects throughout American history of migration to and within the United States, both on the place of origin and destination. Antebellum immigration (8-O.4) Cities in the Gilded Age (8-R.2)
item_specs: Assessment items should analyze the effects of migration on both the place of origin and destination throughout American history. Items may include research projects, essays, and geographic analysis.
essential_questions: How has migration shaped the cultural landscape of the United States?
What are the historical impacts of migration on different regions?
In what ways do geographic tools help us understand migration patterns?
learning_targets: I can analyze the effects of migration on cultural and geographic changes.
I can use geographic tools to map migration patterns.
I can evaluate the historical significance of migration events.
sample_assessment: What was one major effect of migration during the Gilded Age on urban areas? A) Decreased population B) Cultural diversity C) Economic decline D) Rural expansion

benchmark: Use geographic terms and tools to explain cultural diffusion throughout the United States as it expanded its territory.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to explain cultural diffusion as the United States expanded its territory. Items may include case studies, map analysis, and essays discussing the impact of cultural exchanges.
essential_questions: How has territorial expansion influenced cultural diffusion in the U.S.?
What are examples of cultural diffusion resulting from migration?
In what ways do geographic tools help illustrate cultural changes?
learning_targets: I can explain how cultural diffusion occurs during territorial expansion.
I can identify examples of cultural exchange in U.S. history.
I can use geographic tools to analyze cultural diffusion.
sample_assessment: Which of the following is an example of cultural diffusion in the United States? A) The establishment of national parks B) The spread of jazz music from New Orleans C) The construction of highways D) The founding of new states

benchmark: Interpret databases, case studies, and maps to describe the role that regions play in influencing trade, migration patterns, and cultural/political interaction in North America throughout time.
item_specs: Students use multiple sources to analyze how regions influence trade and cultural interaction over time.
essential_questions: How have regions influenced trade and migration in North America?
How do regions interact culturally and politically?
learning_targets: I can use maps to trace trade routes in North America.
I can explain how regional differences drive migration patterns.
I can analyze cultural and political interactions between regions.
sample_assessment: The development of railroads in the 1800s MOST affected regional interaction by: A) Reducing trade B) Connecting regions for faster trade and migration C) Isolating communities D) Replacing water transportation entirely

benchmark: Use geographic terms and tools to analyze case studies of the development, growth, and changing nature of cities and urban centers in the United States over time.
item_specs: Assessment items should require students to analyze case studies of urban centers, utilizing geographic terms and tools. Items may include interpreting maps, graphs, or data sets, and making connections between urban growth and historical events.
essential_questions: How have geographic factors influenced the growth of cities in the United States?
What tools can we use to analyze urban development over time?
In what ways do case studies help us understand the changing nature of urban centers?
learning_targets: I can identify key geographic terms related to urban development.
I can analyze case studies to understand the growth of cities.
I can use geographic tools to interpret data about urban centers.
sample_assessment: Which geographic factor most significantly contributed to the growth of New York City in the 19th century? A) Climate B) Natural resources C) Transportation networks D) Political stability

benchmark: Use political maps to describe changes in boundaries and governance throughout American history. The Louisiana Purchase (8-M.7) Texas independence and annexation (8-O.7) Causes of the Civil War: Missouri Compromise to Bleeding Kansas (8-O.9)
item_specs: Assessment items should focus on students' ability to interpret political maps and understand historical changes in boundaries and governance. Items may include map analysis, identifying key events, and explaining their significance in American history.
essential_questions: How have political boundaries changed throughout American history?
What role did key events play in shaping governance in the United States?
How can maps help us understand historical changes in territory?
learning_targets: I can describe changes in political boundaries using maps.
I can explain the significance of historical events on governance.
I can analyze political maps to identify key changes over time.
sample_assessment: What was a significant outcome of the Louisiana Purchase in terms of political boundaries? A) It doubled the size of the United States B) It led to the Civil War C) It established new governance structures D) It ended westward expansion

benchmark: Describe human dependence on the physical environment and natural resources to satisfy basic needs in local environments in the United States.
item_specs: Students describe how humans depend on natural resources and the physical environment.
essential_questions: How do humans depend on the physical environment?
What natural resources are essential for human survival?
learning_targets: I can describe how humans depend on natural resources.
I can explain how the physical environment affects daily life.
I can identify renewable and nonrenewable resources.
sample_assessment: Which is an example of a nonrenewable resource? A) Solar energy B) Wind C) Coal D) Timber

benchmark: Describe the impact of human modifications on the physical environment and ecosystems of the United States throughout history.
item_specs: Students describe how human activities have modified the environment throughout American history.
essential_questions: How have humans modified the environment in the United States?
What are the consequences of environmental modification?
learning_targets: I can identify ways humans have modified the environment.
I can describe the impact of industrialization on ecosystems.
I can analyze the consequences of environmental changes.
sample_assessment: The construction of the Transcontinental Railroad in the 1860s led to which environmental impact? A) Increased biodiversity B) Destruction of buffalo habitat and grasslands C) Cleaner waterways D) Reduced deforestation

benchmark: Use appropriate maps and other graphic representations to analyze geographic problems and changes over time.
item_specs: Students use maps and graphics to analyze geographic problems and historical changes.
essential_questions: How can maps help us understand geographic problems?
How has the geographic landscape changed over time?
learning_targets: I can use maps to analyze geographic problems.
I can compare maps from different time periods.
I can identify geographic changes over time.
sample_assessment: Comparing a map of the United States in 1800 to one in 1900 would BEST show: A) Changes in climate B) Westward expansion of territories and states C) Shifts in ocean currents D) Changes in mountain elevations

benchmark: Illustrate places and events in U.S. history through the use of narratives and graphic representations.
item_specs: Students create and interpret narratives and graphics that illustrate historical events and places.
essential_questions: How can maps and graphics help tell the story of American history?
What role does place play in understanding historical events?
learning_targets: I can create graphic representations of historical events.
I can use narratives to explain geographic significance.
I can connect places to important events in U.S. history.
sample_assessment: A map showing the route of Lewis and Clark's expedition is an example of: A) A political map B) A graphic representation of a historical event C) A physical map D) A population density map
